docs.title: 🛠️ Aletheia
docs.text: Welcome to your local research & engineering wiki. This is your central hub for technical guides, infrastructure notes, and lab protocols.

docs.title: 🚀 Quick Access Dashboard
docs.text: Category Description Link 📂 Data Sync Google Drive & rclone setup (personal keys) Rclone Guide 📊 Monitoring Performance monitoring & shortcuts Monitoring Guide 🧊 Palmetto HPC Clemson HPC connection and usage Palmetto Guide 🐍 Python Environment management and Conda tips Python Guide

docs.title: 🧠 Knowledge Map
docs.text:
graph TD
    Works["Research Workspace"]
    Wiki["Aletheia Wiki"]
    Sync["Data Sync (Rclone)"]
    Mon["System Monitoring"]
    Lab["Research Projects"]

    Works --> Wiki
    Works --> Lab
    Wiki --> Sync
    Wiki --> Mon

docs.title: 📖 How to use this Wiki
docs.text:
• Navigation: Click the links above to jump to specific guides.
  • Search: Use Ctrl+Shift+F in VS Code to search across all your documentation.
  • Editing: Just edit any Markdown file. Your changes are saved instantly!

[!TIP] Keep this folder synced with Google Drive using the rclone commands in the Data Sync guide to ensure your wiki is backed up!

docs.title: 🛠️ Maintenance
docs.text:
• Start the Server: Just type wiki in any terminal!
  • Update Content: Just edit the files in the docs/ folder. The website updates automatically!

Last updated: 2026-03-03

docs.title: 🐙 Git & GitHub Workflow Guide
docs.text: This guide documents the exact workflow we used to secure, version-control, and host the Aletheia Wiki.

docs.title: 1. Initializing the Repository
docs.text:
To turn a local folder into a Git repository: 

cd ~/works/wiki
git init

docs.title: 2. Connecting to GitHub (gh CLI)
docs.text: We used the GitHub CLI to handle authentication and repository creation without leaving the terminal.

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text:
gh auth login
Selection during setup: SSH, Web Browser login.

docs.title: Creating the Project
docs.text:
gh repo create aletheia --public --source=. --remote=origin --push
- --public: Makes the code visible to everyone. - --source=.: Uses the current folder as the local source. - --push: Uploads the code immediately.

docs.title: Updating Metadata (About Section)
docs.text:
gh repo edit paulshamrat/aletheia \
  --description "A curated technical knowledge base..." \
  --homepage "https://paulshamrat.github.io/aletheia/"

docs.title: 3. The Branching Strategy
docs.text:
We use two main branches to keep the source code and the website separate:

  1. main branch: Contains your Markdown source files. This is what you edit.
  2. gh-pages branch: Contains the built HTML files. This is what the public sees as a website.

docs.title: Switching Branches
docs.text:
git checkout main      # Back to editing mode
git checkout gh-pages  # View the built website files

docs.title: 4. Hosting on GitHub Pages
docs.text: To update your website after making changes to your Markdown files:

docs.title: The Manual (Safe) Way
docs.text:
# 1. Build the site
mkdocs build 

# 2. Deploy to the special gh-pages branch
mkdocs gh-deploy

docs.title: 5. Security & Privacy
docs.text: This is the most critical part of our setup. We use three layers of security to keep your personal data private.

docs.title: Layer 1: .gitignore
docs.text:
Prevents Git from ever "seeing" certain files. 

# .gitignore content
docs/internal/    # Your private notes folder
site/             # The built website (we only push it to gh-pages)
*.log             # System logs

docs.title: Layer 2: MkDocs Snippets
docs.text:
Allows you to keep a guide public but "punch out" a hole for private data. 

# In your guide:
- Local: MkDocs fills the hole with your secret file. - GitHub: The hole remains empty (invisible).

docs.title: Layer 3: exclude_docs (Build Security)
docs.text:
Prevents private files from being accidentally included in the website bundle during mkdocs build. 

# mkdocs.yml
exclude_docs: |
  internal/*

docs.title: 6. Daily Commands Summary
docs.text: Action Command Save Changes git add . && git commit -m "Your message" Upload Source git push origin main Update Website mkdocs gh-deploy Start Local View wiki (custom alias)

docs.title: Linux System Performance Monitoring
docs.text: This guide covers how to set up Windows-like system monitoring on Ubuntu (GNOME).

docs.title: Summary Table
docs.text: Feature Tool Shortcut / Access Main Interface GNOME System Monitor Ctrl+Shift+Esc Tray Icons Vitals (GNOME Extension) Top Panel Advanced CLI btop Terminal (btop) Dock Alignment Dash to Panel Absolute Centering

docs.title: 1. Keyboard Shortcut (Ctrl+Shift+Esc)
docs.text:
The default task manager in Ubuntu is gnome-system-monitor. To map it to the familiar Windows shortcut:

  1. Open Settings > Keyboard > View and Customize Shortcuts.
  2. Select Custom Shortcuts.
  3. Add a new shortcut:
    • Name: System Monitor
    • Command: gnome-system-monitor
    • Shortcut: Ctrl+Shift+Esc

docs.title: 2. Real-time Monitoring (Top Bar)
docs.text:
To see CPU, RAM, and Temperature at a glance in your top panel:

  1. Install Extension Manager: 
    sudo apt update && sudo apt install -y gnome-shell-extension-manager
  2. Open Extension Manager.
  3. Go to the Browse tab.
  4. Search for and install Vitals.
  5. Once active, your performance metrics will appear in the top panel.

docs.title: 3. High-Performance Terminal Monitor (btop)
docs.text: If you prefer a terminal-based interface, btop is highly recommended for its visual aesthetics and detailed information.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: sudo apt install -y btop

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Run btop in any terminal. Use q or Esc to quit.

docs.title: 4. Centering the Dock (Windows 11 Style)
docs.text:
If the dock icons are shifted to the left, you can force them to stay in the exact center of the screen:

  1. Right-click on any empty space in the taskbar.
  2. Select "Dash to Panel Settings".
  3. Navigate to Position > Taskbar Display.
  4. Set "Center Taskbar" to Absolute.

This ensures the dock uses the monitor's center point rather than centering relative to the available space between other elements.

docs.title: Resource Impact
docs.text: These tools are designed to be lightweight: - Vitals: Extremely low (~2-5MB RAM). - btop: Low (~10-20MB RAM). - System Monitor: Moderate (~40-80MB RAM), only while open.

docs.title: 🏔️ Palmetto HPC Guide
docs.text: This guide covers usage and tips for the Palmetto High-Performance Computing (HPC) cluster at Clemson University.

docs.title: 1. Connecting to Palmetto
docs.text:
Use the following command to SSH into the cluster: 

ssh shamrap@slogin.palmetto.clemson.edu

docs.title: 2. Interactive Jobs (PBS)
docs.text:
To request an interactive node with specific resources: 

qsub -I -l select=1:ncpus=4:mem=16gb,walltime=02:00:00

docs.title: 3. Data Transfer
docs.text:
Use rclone (if configured on Palmetto) or scp to move files: 

scp -r ./local_folder shamrap@slogin.palmetto.clemson.edu:/home/shamrap/destination

docs.title: 4. Useful Commands
docs.text:
• qstat -u shamrap: Check your running jobs.
  • checknode: See available resources.
  • module list: Show loaded software modules.

docs.title: 🐍 Python & Conda Management
docs.text: Best practices for managing environments and dependencies for your research.

docs.title: 1. Basic Environment Setup
docs.text:
Always create a dedicated environment for each project: 

conda create -n my_project python=3.10
conda activate my_project

docs.title: 2. Installing Packages
docs.text:
Prefer using conda for data science packages and pip for everything else: 

conda install numpy pandas matplotlib
pip install -r requirements.txt

docs.title: 3. Saving & Exporting
docs.text:
To share or backup your environment: 

conda env export > environment.yml

docs.title: 4. Common Fixes
docs.text:
• Slow Solves: Use mamba instead of conda for much faster package resolution.
  • Kernel Not Found: If your environment isn't showing up in Jupyter: 
    pip install ipykernel
python -m ipykernel install --user --name my_project

docs.title: Rclone Google Drive Setup & Sync Guide
docs.text: This is your personal guide for managing Google Drive synchronization on this machine.

docs.title: 1. Credentials (Your Private Tunnel)
docs.text: We have configured rclone to use your own Google Cloud Project. This stops the "daily logout" issue.

docs.title: How to create your own Client ID & Secret
docs.text:
If you ever need to create a new set of keys from scratch, follow these steps:

  1. Google Cloud Console: Go to the Google Cloud Console.
  2. Create Project: Click the project dropdown at the top and select "New Project". Give it a name like rclone-paul.
  3. Enable API: In the search bar, type "Google Drive API" and click Enable.
  4. Configure OAuth Consent:
    • Go to APIs & Services > OAuth consent screen.
    • Choose "External" and click Create.
    • Fill in the App name (e.g., rclone) and your User support email.
    • Add your email to the Developer contact information.
    • Click Save and Continue until you reach the dashboard.
  5. Create Credentials:
    • Go to APIs & Services > Credentials.
    • Click + Create Credentials > OAuth client ID.
    • Select "Desktop app" as the Application type.
    • Name it rclone-paul and click Create.
  6. Get the Keys: A popup will show your Client ID and Client Secret. Copy these into this guide!

docs.title: How to re-authenticate (if needed)
docs.text:
If you ever get an "Authorization failed" error, run: 

rclone config reconnect gdrive:
1. Type y to refresh the token. 2. Type y for auto-config. 3. In the browser, log in with shamrap@g.clemson.edu. 4. Click Advanced > Go to rclone (unsafe) > Allow.

docs.title: 2. Daily Workflow (Your Duty)
docs.text: To keep your files synced, you should follow this routine: - After significant local changes: Run the "Upload" command to back up your work. - Before starting work: Run the "Download" command if you think you changed files on another device.

docs.title: Step 1: Clean Up Duplicates
docs.text:
Google Drive sometimes creates multiple files with the same name. Run this to fix it automatically: 

rclone dedupe gdrive:works --dedupe-mode largest -P

docs.title: Step 2: Sync Your Work
docs.text:
• To Upload (Local -> Cloud): 
    rclone copy /home/paul/works gdrive:works -P
  • To Download (Cloud -> Local): 
    rclone copy gdrive:works /home/paul/works -P

Note: I recommend using copy instead of sync. copy is safer because it never deletes files; it only adds new ones.

docs.title: 4. Speed Tips (Save Time)
docs.text: If checking all 30,000 files takes too long (e.g., 4-5 minutes), use these tricks:

docs.title: Trick 1: Sync Only Your Active Project
docs.text:
Instead of syncing all of works, only sync the folder you are actually using today: 

rclone copy ~/works/research-lab/YOUR_PROJECT gdrive:works/research-lab/YOUR_PROJECT -P

docs.title: Trick 2: Use Fast-List
docs.text:
This tells Google Drive to send the file list in larger chunks, which can cut the "Checking" time in half: 

rclone copy /home/paul/works gdrive:works --fast-list -P

docs.title: 5. Helpful Tips
docs.text:
• Check first: Run rclone check /home/paul/works gdrive:works --size-only to see what is different without changing anything.
  • Progress: Always use -P to see how much data is being transferred.
